| Chipotle Pepper | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Category | Herbs and spices |

Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients

The **chipotle pepper** is a smoked and dried ripe jalapeño pepper.**[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)**[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) It can come in different forms for use in cooking.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

![](/wiki/File:Chile_Morita_Mexicano.jpg)

Morita chipotles

All chipotles are wrinkled and have a smoky, medium spicy flavor with a hint of fruity sweetness.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[4]](#cite_note-4) Two primary varieties of chipotle exist: morita and meco.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Moritas are dark red with a pliable texture similar to that of a raisin.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) They are not smoked as long, which gives them a milder flavor.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) On the other hand, mecos are smoked for much longer, which turns them brown and gives them an intensity of flavor.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

![](/wiki/File:Chipotlesenadobo.jpg)

Chipotles in adobo

Once dried, chipotles may be processed in a few ways. They can be sold whole or ground to flakes or powder.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) They may also be be stewed in adobo sauce—a blend of tomato, vinegar, and spices—and canned to make a versatile condiment.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) When rehydrated, they can be blended to make a chipotle purée.

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

As a dried food, chipotles keep well at room temperature when kept away from light, heat, air, and moisture.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) Under ideal conditions, they can last for a couple years.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) Canned chiles in adobo can last for days to weeks in the fridge after opening.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)

## Preparation

[edit | edit source]

Before using chipotles, remove the stems, and discard the seeds if desired to reduce the bitterness of the chile.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) If you want to add additional char and flavor, you can dry toast them in a skillet before proceeding.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) From here, you can grind or rehydrate them.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) To rehydrate, cut a little slit in the sides and soak in hot water—or another liquid—for several minutes.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Chipotles are commonly used in Mexican cuisine, as well as Tex-Mex and United States cuisines.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) The ground powder makes a good component of a meat rub.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) Chipotles in adobo are excellent as part of condiments and marinades, where the adobo adds a tang and the chile adds spice and smoke.[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)
